message.content:
The fog on Blackwood Rock didn’t just obscure vision; it swallowed sound. For Elias Thorne, the lighthouse keeper, the silence was a companion he had learned to respect, if not love. For thirty years, he had climbed the spiral stairs, polished the Fresnel lens, and watched the churning Atlantic, waiting for ships that never came near enough to need his warning.

It was a Tuesday, the kind of gray, oppressive day that made the white stone of the lighthouse feel like a tomb. Elias was sweeping the gallery deck when a dull *thud* against the railing broke the monotony.

He paused, broom in hand. There, wedged between the iron bars, was a bottle. Not the cheap, mass-produced kind found in souvenir shops, but an old, thick-glass vessel, green with age and encrusted with barnacles. The cork was sealed with wax, stamped with a symbol Elias didn’t recognize: a circle intersected by a single vertical line.

Curiosity, a dangerous thing for a man who preferred routine, got the better of him. He pried the bottle free. It was heavy. Inside, a tube of parchment was rolled tight.

Back in his small, oil-stained kitchen, Elias heated a knife to melt the wax. His hands trembled slightly—not from cold, but from the sheer improbability of the moment. He slid the paper out. It was brittle, yellowed, but the ink was dark and sharp.

*To the Keeper of Blackwood,*

*If you are reading this, the cycle has reset. I am you. I am writing this from the future, or perhaps the past—time is a loop here, not a line. The light you tend does not guide ships. It guides *them* back.*
